[[RunningHead]]

Ein Krankenbericht: Als Tiefenpsychologischer Beitrag Zum Problem Einer Geistigpolitischen Neuorientierung

('A Case-report: A Contribution of Depth Psychology to the Problem of a Politicospiritual Reorientation.') By Wilhelm Laiblin. Heidelberg: Verlag Lambert Schneider, 1949.

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer

Hedwig Hoffer  

The treatment is based on C. G. Jung's teachings. The patient, a German soldier twenty-one years of age, came home from the war completely blind, and still with the typical Nazi mentality. The dreams of the patient form the main part of the material. His dreams are discussed with the dreamer and by a kind of re-education a complete change of heart and an adjustment to his physical condition is brought about.

What the author calls dreams seems to the reviewer a much censored, rehearsed and elaborated concoction of genuine dream-elements with conscious and preconscious thoughts. Scanty detailed knowledge of the dreamer's personal history and only few associations are needed for the dream interpretations. The collective, not the individual, unconscious captivates the interpreter's interest. Many of the interpretations are in the reviewer's view mere amplifications in the Jungian sense and not interpretations of the specific dream at all.

294

Therese Benedek, M.D.
Chicago

Therese Benedek  

The aim of this book is to pass on the 'insight' of the anthropologist to those who are not acquainted with the work of scientific anthropology. Its purpose is more than informative; it is propagandist in the best sense of the word. The author, committed to the belief that more knowledge means more freedom, attempts to convey her knowledge of the rôle which 'maleness and femaleness' play in cultural processes to a broad audience. She firmly believes that such knowledge, integrated in the 'mind of the nation', will eventually influence society to make the best use of the specific endowments of both sexes, and thus help mankind in 'rising to heights of which each living generation can only glimpse the next step of the ascent'.

The book is divided into four parts.

211

responseSet.title: Psycho-Analysis and the Form-Interpretation Test
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psycho-Analysis and the Form-Interpretation Test11

Hans Zulliger

Hans Zulliger

Hans Zulliger
ITTIGEN, BERNE

Hans Zulliger  

Certain Rorschach responses are of interest to the psycho-analytically-oriented Rorschach worker in that their content seems to be a symbolic representation of unconscious mental processes. These responses appear to represent wish-fulfilments in the same way as dreams and symptoms, and are often extremely well constructed from the raw material of the blot.

The interpretation of such symbolic content in addition to the classical Rorschach assessment of the test-results affords a deeper insight into the subject's unconscious mental life. The investigator should be careful, however, not to interpret these responses purely by analogy with dream material, and to guard against the temptations of 'wild' psycho-analysis. Both the Rorschach and the Behn-Rorschach (Bero) blots2 should be used, for it has been found that subjects will follow a certain 'inner sequence' in their responses to each test. Information from one test will confirm and supplement the findings of the other. In addition the Z-test3 has been found useful in supplying indications of underlying complexes.

If the subject has established a good transference relationship to the investigator, then it may be possible to probe a little further, and careful questioning may elicit relevant information directly from the subject. Often, too, his relatives can be helpful, though the best validation of the test findings would come through the psycho-analysis of the subject.

Even in those cases where the investigator is asked for advice and guidance, and not for an extensive psychological report, such an analysis of test content will materially assist him in understanding the patient's difficulties, and will therefore enable him to give more appropriate advice.

I should like to show, with an actual example, the value of psycho-analytical investigation of certain response contents, over and above the usual formal Rorschach interpretation. The case is one of a fifteen-year-old girl, whom we shall call Edith, and who was in a class being tested for control purposes. She gave the following unusual and interesting responses to the Z-test.

<artinfo arttype="ART" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ART" id="IJP.031.0125A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1950</artyear><artvol actual="31">31</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">125-131</artpgrg><arttitle>Functional Aspects of the Mental Apparatus<ftnx r="F001" type="PG">1</ftnx></arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Glover, Edward" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Edward</nfirst><nlast>Glover</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: Functional Aspects of the Mental Apparatus
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Functional Aspects of the Mental Apparatus11

Edward Glover

Edward Glover

Edward Glover
LONDON

Edward Glover  

At the Amsterdam Congress of European Analysts I endeavoured to outline the most important of those sparingly stated basic concepts on which the theory and practice of psycho-analysis is founded and on which the validity of the most elaborate presentation of mental structure ultimately depends.2 On that occasion I pointed out that Freud's original description of the mental apparatus, as outlined in the theoretical part of his Interpretation of Dreams, provided us with a perfectly adequate reconstruction of the first stages of mental life. It is with this frequently misunderstood but nevertheless master-concept of a mental apparatus that I am concerned here.

The fact that in recent years psycho-analysts have neglected the concept of a mental apparatus is due to a number of causes some of which cannot be distinguished from resistances. Chief of these is an inveterate tendency to anthropomorphize all mental concepts. Because, for example, we tend to identify ourselves with our ego, or, as the case may be, our super-ego, it is easy to neglect the purely functional aspects of these psychic institutions as instruments of adaptation. This tendency to anthropomorphize the mental apparatus, to personalize its parts and functions, was accelerated by the circumstance that Freud's original and mainly dynamic concept of the apparatus was considerably overlaid by his later expansions of structural psychology. This emphasis on ego-psychology has given rise during the past ten to fifteen years to attempts to describe early functional (dynamic) phases of the mental apparatus in terms of organized ego-institutions; or, to put the matter more simply, to attribute to the suckling the conscious mentality and unconscious organization of a four-year-old child.

Similarly, concepts of unconscious conflict appropriate to comparatively late symptom-formations such as the infantile psycho-neuroses, tend to be applied to earlier forms of developmental disorder; for example, to rudimentary psycho-somatic reactions, to convulsive seizures and to the essential (infantile) psychoses. We are inclined, that is to say, to look upon the earliest disturbances of mental function as if their etiology did not differ in any essential respect from the etiology of the psycho-neuroses.

responseSet.title: Myths of Middle India: By Verrier Elwin.  (London:  Geoffrey Cumberlege.  Oxford University Press.  Pp. xvi + 532.  Price 25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Myths of Middle India

By Verrier Elwin. (London: Geoffrey Cumberlege. Oxford University Press. Pp. xvi + 532. Price 25 s.)

Masud Khan

Masud Khan

Masud Khan
London

Masud Khan  

In this book Dr. Elwin has detailed the oral literature of a very vast and complex cultural area, and has combed it with such thoroughness that the result is almost perplexing in its richness of yield. Material from nearly forty-two tribes has been collected, stretching from the Central Provinces to Western Orissa. The book is divided into four parts: Man and His Universe, The Natural World, Human Life and Human Institutions. Each myth or story is carefully related to its tribal setting and the geographical area. The chief merit of the book is, perhaps, in its authentic and lively translation of this oral literature into cold print and a foreign language. The problems surmounted here can hardly be over-estimated, because this oral literature is expressed in languages that are intimately interwoven with the psycho-physical habits, experiences, and peculiarities of these people—as is amply evidenced by the content of their myths. Dr. Elwin has at times preferred to retain the flavour and feel of the original idiom and metaphor even when it has made the translation entirely fantastic.

It is almost impossible to indicate the richness of the material contained: it ranges from the myths and stories about creation and the universe to those explaining tears and laughter. The functions and activities of the human body, the birth and existence of animals, the origin of religious institutions, magic, dance, etc., all are detailed as reflected in the phantasies of these people.

To the readers of this Journal the long chapter on 'Psycho-pathology: the Vagina Dentata Legend' may be of special interest. The section on the Human Body is introduced by Dr. Elwin with a note of caution to the 'fastidious reader' and it is one of the many merits of this book that Dr. Elwin does not flinch on any prudish grounds from detailing his material. As he puts it: 'To the aboriginal—let us be frank—the most important thing about the human body is its sexual apparatus; it is this on which he lavishes his most tender fancies; it is this which excites his most eager interest in narrative'.

W. Clifford Scott

W. Clifford Scott  

Psycho-analysts will be indebted to a member of the Indian Society, Mr. Ghosh, for a revision of Dr. Basu's translation of this Sanskrit work which dates from before the fifth century A.D.

Every-day Psychoanalysis. By G. S. Bose. (Calcutta: Susil Gupta Publishing Co., 1945. Pp. 280. R.F. 6.)

This popular introduction to psycho-analysis by the former President of the Indian Society begins with a general statement regarding modern psychology. It ends with a historical description of psycho-analysis, the psycho-analytic view of normality, the psycho-analytic claims with regard to treatment and an outline biography of Sigmund Freud. In the body of the book the analytic interpretation of choice of work, normal mood variations, childhood development, crime, dreams, marital difficulties in general and sexual difficulties in particular are discussed in a manner suited to those with no previous acquaintance with psychology or psycho-analysis.

215

responseSet.title: Studies in Psychosomatic Medicine. An Approach to the Cause and Treatment of Vegetative Disturbances: By Franz Alexander, M.D., and Thomas Morton French, M.D.  (New York:  The Ronald Press Company, 1948.  Pp. 568.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Studies in Psychosomatic Medicine. An Approach to the Cause and Treatment of Vegetative Disturbances

By Franz Alexander, M.D., and Thomas Morton French, M.D. (New York: The Ronald Press Company, 1948. Pp. 568.)

Paul Goolker

Paul Goolker

Paul Goolker  

This is a collection of previously published papers and is based largely upon the psycho-analytic study of patients suffering from chronic disturbances of the vegetative organs. The term psychosomatic is used as a methodological concept which aims at studying and treating simultaneously psychological and somatic factors in their mutual interrelation. It is repeatedly stressed that there is an intimate affinity between certain emotional constellations (nuclear conflicts) and somatic responses, i.e. etiologic specificity. The vegetative neuroses are distinguished sharply from hysterical conversions; in the former the somatic symptoms are not substitute expressions of repressed emotions, but are normal physiological accompaniments of the emotion. It is only the chronicity of the emotional ension which leads in the latter to a morbid state. Thus in a gastric neurosis the repressed need to be taken care of—to be nursed—leads to certain vegetative responses manifested by secretory and motor changes.

The need to postulate and consider additional factors besides the specific emotional constellation is constantly in sight.

215

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="OBI" j="IJP" artsource="A1V2" arttype="PRO" id="IJP.031.0286A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1950</artyear><artvol actual="31">31</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">286-288</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">William Henry Butter Stoddart, M.D., Lond., F.R.C.P., Lond</arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="R, J." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>J.</nfirst><nlast>R.</nlast></aut>
</artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: William Henry Butter Stoddart, M.D., Lond., F.R.C.P., Lond
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

William Henry Butter Stoddart, M.D., Lond., F.R.C.P., Lond

J. R.

J. R.

J. R.  

A field of human activity such as psycho-dynamics, if it is to develop smoothly and satisfactorily, requires a fairly wide range of personalities amongst its pioneers; it is not necessary that each one should have all of those characteristics which favour the spread of the ideas, but such of the necessary characteristics as they possess must be pronounced and effective.

Stoddart was not a man of original thought, he did not, that is to say, alter the horizon of man's vision, but he was a man of courage, of good nature, of great clinical gifts and of utter common sense. Having these qualities he was able to give much to psycho-analysis in England at a time when such help was most needed.

In many respects he was not the kind of person one would associate with a pioneering spirit. He was placid, seemed to be lazy, he was fat, affable and always well dressed, he enjoyed good food, good wine (or rather champagne) and idle days on the river. He had a pleasant singing voice, enjoyed charades and organizing impromptu concerts and played the piano charmingly. Nothing was done to excess: he was good company (not 'extremely good company'), he wore his clothes with elegance but without ostentation; his cigar case, match box, cigar clipper, all of gold, even his diamond ring, were part of an easy existence and were engulfed and hardly noticed in the portly urbanity that was the essential Stoddart.

Now let us turn to the Biographical Data for the two decades preceding his great Morrison Lectures. They were twenty years of unbroken success, he seemed to have whatever he wanted and to get it without trouble. In 1915 he gave 'The Morrison Lectures' at the Royal College of Physicians, Edinburgh, on 'The New Psychiatry'. These made a furore and, as sometimes happens with furores, they made history. The Challenger—the pupil of Hughlings Jackson, the Lecturer in the Faculty of Medicine, London University, Professor of Psychological Medicine to the Royal Army College—could not easily be put aside.

responseSet.documentRef: Thomas, R. (1950) Oedipus Myth and Complex: By Patrick Mullahy.  (New York:  Hermitage Press, Inc.  Pp. 538.  London:  Allen & Unwin, 1950.  Price 37 s. 6 d.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  31:213-214
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Thomas, R. (1950) Oedipus Myth and Complex: By Patrick Mullahy. (New York: Hermitage Press, Inc. Pp. 538. London: Allen & Unwin, 1950. Price 37 s. 6 d.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 31:213-214

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195031213-214Oedipus Myth and ComplexBy Patrick Mullahy. (New York: Hermitage Press, Inc. Pp. 538. London: Allen & Unwin, 1950. Price 37 s. 6 d.)RuthThomas Ruth ThomasLondon
responseSet.title: Oedipus Myth and Complex: By Patrick Mullahy.  (New York:  Hermitage Press, Inc.  Pp. 538.  London:  Allen & Unwin, 1950.  Price 37 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Oedipus Myth and Complex

By Patrick Mullahy. (New York: Hermitage Press, Inc. Pp. 538. London: Allen & Unwin, 1950. Price 37 s. 6 d.)

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas
London

Ruth Thomas  

This book purports to give an account of the theory of the Oedipus Complex as discovered and developed by Freud, and of the most important modifications to his theory made by leading 'psychoanalysts' since his time. There are four admirably concise and comprehensive chapters setting out the stages by which Freud developed his libido theory and theory of the neuroses, with extensive quotations and references to the main translated works and papers. The theories of Adler, Jung, Rank, Horney, Fromm and Harry Stack Sullivan (of the William Alanson White Psychiatric Foundation, Washington), follow in separate chapters. Finally, the author attempts a critical survey of the seven theories, and the last 150 pages are devoted to a translation of Sophocles' Oedipus trilogy.

In the first part of the book there are passing references to Abraham, Hans Sachs and Ernest Jones among the classical analysts, and of the many singular omissions in a bibliography of seven pages, there is no reference to the work of Ferenczi, Anna Freud or Melanie Klein. This is therefore a book about Freud and those who have seceded from his theories, and if this were clearly stated there could be no objection to it. The title is misleading, as the Oedipus complex dwindles visibly en route till in the work of Dr. Sullivan it appears merely as a statement that the parent, feeling a sense of strangeness with the child of the opposite sex, tends to treat him with 'kid gloves', so that in this relative freedom from authoritarian pressure, the child feels greater affection in return.

There is, however, an underlying theme which is not without interest for the psycho-analyst. All six deviators from Freud's findings agree in abandoning the theory of instinct, 'the cumbersome libido theory', and in playing down the rôle of infantile sexuality, or completely eliminating it as a serious factor in psychological development. They are agreed also that the mother-child relationship is the operative factor in development failure, conceiving the relationship in a wide nurtural sense and not in terms of specific instinctual care. Sociological and environmental influences affecting the child directly or through the parents' methods of education are stressed, education being conceived as influence over the entire pre-adult years. At the same time there is general agreement to abandon the concept of the super-ego as a structured internalization of these influences related to the resolution of the Oedipus complex. Dr. Sullivan's account of the acquisition of social attitudes through empathy in the acquirement of language is the most elaborate and systematized attempt to produce a new dynamic of the process of internalization.

responseSet.title: Basic Principles of Psychoanalysis: By A. A. Brill.  With an introduction by Philip R. Lehrman.  (Doubleday & Co., New York.  Pp. xv and 298.  Price, $3.45.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Basic Principles of Psychoanalysis

By A. A. Brill. With an introduction by Philip R. Lehrman. (Doubleday & Co., New York. Pp. xv and 298. Price, $3.45.)

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer

Mark Kanzer  

Brill's Basic Principles of Psychoanalysis first appeared in 1921. This book, couched in simple terms and taken from material used in lectures to psychologists and educators, represents another of Dr. Brill's indefatigable efforts to arouse interest and spread information about psycho-analysis in ever widening circles. In conception and approach, the work shows the strong influence of Freud's General Introduction to Psychoanalysis which had appeared a short time previously. Chapters on the cathartic method, the symptom, psychopathology of everyday life, wit and dreams comprise the great body of the book and succeed admirably in conveying clearly to lay readers the fundamental ideas of Freud. Cases cited from the vast personal experience of Dr. Brill illustrate, in the earthy and witty way which he made familiar to two generations of students, the meaning and application of psycho-analytic doctrines. In chapters on 'The Only Child' and on 'Fairy Tales and Artistic Productions, ' the author finds the opportunity to record his own personal observations and opinions with regard to the upbringing of children. Finally, in a discussion on 'The Selection of Vocations', there are valuable comments on unconscious factors involved in the choice of a career.

It lends a touching and symbolic note to this work to learn that on the occasion of his departure for the hospital during his last illness, Dr. Brill took the manuscript of the book with him in order to make the final corrections. When it became apparent that the task lay beyond his strength, he requested that it be carried on by his colleague, Dr. Lehrman, who fulfilled this wish and furnished the book with an introduction.

216

<artinfo lang="en" artsubtype="ART" j="IJP" artsource="A1V2" arttype="ART" id="IJP.031.0008A" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1950</artyear><artvol actual="31">31</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">8-11</artpgrg><arttitle lang="en">Illness Following Dreams<ftnx r="F001" type="PG">1</ftnx></arttitle><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Bartemeier, Leo H." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Leo</nfirst><nmid>H.</nmid><nlast>Bartemeier</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: Illness Following Dreams
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Illness Following Dreams11

Leo Bartemeier

Leo Bartemeier

Leo H. Bartemeier
DETROIT

Leo Bartemeier  

The occurrence of acute reactions in the wake of certain dreams is a phenomenon for which no satisfactory explanation has been offered, although it is a common experience in psychoanalytic therapy. These reactions vary in their intensity, their nature and their duration. They include transitory or prolonged illnesses which appear immediately on awakening from a dream or they may not occur until several hours after a night's sleep. If the patient remembers his dream he frequently tells us that his symptom is caused by it or connected with it. Otherwise he often senses that his distress is somehow related to his sleep. If his complaint does not appear until several hours after sleep and he does not remember having dreamed he has no explanation for the occurrence of his symptom. These are the patients who may consult physicians and undergo various examinations to no avail because the symptoms of which they complain are psychological in origin and are usually transitory in nature.

These acute reactions which occur in connection with dreams are either psychological or somatic and vary from mild and fleeting phenomena to prolonged and incapacitating illnesses. Those which I have observed and which I will discuss include instances of migraine, skin lesions, an acute anxiety state and an agitated depression requiring hospitalization.

In his review of the literature of the dream Freud (1) referred to the investigations of the German, French and Italian psychiatrists who had reported their observations about the relations between dreams and psychoses. He then stated that 'Fere refers to a dream which caused an hysterical paralysis. Here the dream is offered us as the ætiology of mental disturbance, though we equally consider the prevailing conditions when we declare that the mental disturbance shows its first manifestation in dream life, that it has its first outbreak in the dream.' Following Freud's discovery that the same mechanisms are at work in the formation of neurotic symptoms as those which turn the latent dream-thoughts into the manifest dream, psycho-analysts appear to have lost interest in those certain dreams which are followed by acute reactions. That this may be so is illustrated by the fact that the next article on this subject seems not to have appeared until 1910 when Dr. G. A. Waterman (2) wrote about 'dreams as a cause of symptoms.' His patient was a middle-aged woman who remained depressed following the death of her husband. She awakened from sleep on two occasions with severe headache and nausea which persisted for a week. Dr. Waterman recovered her dream under hypnosis. The content clearly disclosed her impulse to suicide by drinking poison. Dr. Waterman incorrectly concluded that her symptoms had been caused by her dream. In 1911 Dr. Ernest Jones (3) spoke about the relation between dreams and neurotic symptoms as follows: 'It is not very rare for a neurotic symptom actually to date from a given dream.' As an instance, he mentioned the case of a patient who, whenever he had a certain dream, to the effect that he was being hanged, always suffered for some time from an hysterical paralysis of the right arm. Dr. Jones was the first investigator to point out that 'it is incorrect to regard the dream as the cause of the symptom that subsequently arises. They both have a common cause in some buried thoughts. The same thoughts can come to expression in both a dream and a neurotic symptom, thus illustrating the near relationship of the two.' Dr. Jones further stated that 'the most that the dream can do is to determine the precise form taken by the symptom in question.' In 1947, Dr. H. I. Kupper (4) wrote about 'some aspects of the dream in psycho-somatic disease.' This was a report of his study of a man who had suffered from abdominal cramps and angioneurotic oedema, and another who had epileptic seizures on awakening from sleep. Dr.

<autaff><addr><ln>N. Lionel Blitzsten</ln><ln>CHIGAGO</ln><ln><cs style="AuthorName">Ruth S. Eissler</cs></ln><ln>NEW YORK</ln><ln><cs style="AuthorName">K. R. Eissler</cs></ln><ln>NEW YORK</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Emergence of Hidden Ego Tendencies During Dream Analysis
responseSet.authorMast: N. Lionel Blitzsten,  Ruth S. Eissler and  K. R. Eissler
responseSet.authorCitation: Blitzsten, N. L., Eissler, R. S. & Eissler, K. R.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Emergence of Hidden Ego Tendencies During Dream Analysis

N. Blitzsten , Ruth Eissler and K. Eissler

K. Eissler

N. Lionel Blitzsten
CHIGAGO
Ruth S. Eissler
NEW YORK
K. R. Eissler
NEW YORK

K. Eissler  

From the early days of psycho-analysis dreams have been one of the main roads to uncovering the unconscious, and no other system of psycho-therapy or psychology has given them the same emphasis or put them to such constructive use.

It became apparent to Freud that the complex configuration of a dream is not a static phenomenon but the end product of various forces striving towards partly identical and partly disparate goals. His disentanglement of these forces, his ability to use the dream as a phenomenon to deduce and describe the forces which must be at work in its production and his reconstruction of what must be the structure of the apparatus which is endowed with the faculty of producing dreams is one of the greatest of intellectual achievements. Rarely was so much concluded from so little by succinct ratiocination and empirical observation. It would seem that little could be added after Freud's exhaustive analysis. And actually, as Freud himself mentioned, little new has been added, although he seems to have thought that our knowledge of dreams and particularly of the dream work is far from complete. With the shift of psycho-analytic interest to ego psychology it was logical that some analysts should scrutinize anew the dream contents for clues to the unconscious structure of the ego. In Freud's first publication, the ego's contribution to dreams was limited to its wish to sleep, to the censoring function, and to the secondary elaboration. In 1923, however, Freud distinguished between 'dreams from above and dreams from below'2 and in 1938 he wrote: 'dreams may arise either from the id or from the ego', 3 thus enlarging the ego's contribution to dreams. Yet from the beginning of the psycho-analytic interest in dreams, attention was paid to another series of dream problems albeit of minor importance, namely the vicissitudes of the dream after awakening. There are two main alternatives. The dream is either forgotten or distorted. Distortion is the engrossment or continuation of that work which had been initiated prior to the dream's appearance in the conscious. The forgetting is the manifest victory of those forces which are called by the general term 'resistance' but which are also actively engaged in dream work.

responseSet.title: 'Mania and Sleep.': Bertram D. Lewin.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Mania and Sleep.'

Bertram D. Lewin.

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser

Sylvan Keiser  

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 1949, Vol. XVIII, p. 419.

Case material is taken from the history of a young lady who had four hypomanic attacks during her analysis. Each was preceded by a blank dream. Before each episode she had lunch and had gone shopping with a mother figure. In the analytic hour she had a blissful mood of erotic absorption. That night she had an orgasm with a blank dream. She awoke with a hypomania. In her clear periods she had dreams in which young men in bed with her turned into her mother. During the hypomania men played a great part, but her mother was taboo as a topic for analysis.

The blank herald dream is to be interpreted not only as a dream of oral satisfaction of fusion with the mother at the breast, but also as picturing most lucidly the fulfilment of the wish to sleep. The patient's hypomanic attacks were like manifest dreams which denied the latent dream thoughts of the day's residual. Features of the manic are compared to mechanisms that are found in dreams. The elated mood of the manic and his happy dreams were long ago shown to hide death wishes or thoughts of one's death. The dreams of romping and playing resemble the innocent games of the hypomanic. Dreams with endless details are compared to the variety of content found in a typical flight of ideas. After discussing varieties of sleep and wakefulness and dreams, mania may be compared to sleep if the kind of sleep is specified.

Both sleep and mania are the result of an intrapsychic fusion with the breast at nursing, but stupor alone reproduces the infant's sound sleep. Elation repeats the wish-fulfilling dreaming sleep of an older child. This special sleep-equivalent is guarded by a censorship that is created to employ the defence mechanism of denial—of insight—and to prevent emotional acceptance.

207

D." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>C.</nfirst><nmid>D.</nmid><nlast>Daly</nlast></aut>
<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">C. D. Daly</cs></ln><ln>LONDON</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psycho-Biological Origins of Circumcision
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psycho-Biological Origins of Circumcision

C. Daly

C. Daly

C. D. Daly
LONDON

C. Daly  

SUMMARY

I would broadly summarize my view of the rôles performed by the puberty rites, in which circumcision is included, as follows. They:

I. Reinforce the repression of patricide, fratricide and incest.

II. Frustrate the narcissistic masturbatory practices which enable both sexes to cling to their incestuous phantasies.

III. Repress both heterosexual and homosexual rivalry.

IV. Promote fertility by counteracting the effects of the primary phylogenetic traumatic repression of incest, i.e. inversion.

V. Diminish, in the case of boys, the enhanced fear of and pathological fixation to the mother, i.e. the maternal authority which exists in the family circle, authority which, to some extent, has its origins in man's unconscious fear of woman, and reinstate patriarchal authority.

This divorce from the mother is achieved by the symbolic father playing the double rôle of the eating, castrating, and impregnating father on the one hand, and on the other of the mother from whom the youth is reborn; reborn, however, with the full status of a man in whom all feminine tendencies have been repressed. The youth's feminine identification with the mother is replaced by masculine identification with the father. (In the case of girls, clitoridectomy has the opposite effect.)

VI. Conscious permanent identification with the circumcised father and brothers of the tribe; continuity in posterity of submission to patriarchal authority and obedience to its will being ensured through the blood covenant.

responseSet.title: 'Clinical Variants of Moral Values.': Gregory Zilboorg, M.D.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Clinical Variants of Moral Values.'

Gregory Zilboorg, M.D.

Martin Grotjahn M.D.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn  

American J. of Psychiatry, 106, 10, April, 1950, pp. 744–47.

Not all sense of guilt is neurotic, and not to feel guilty is not necessarily a sign of normality. The psychiatrist is not concerned primarily with moral problems, but he does not reject them and he cannot and does not function without moral values. Analysts want to relieve their patients of the sense of guilt for things they have never really or wittingly done, but they leave their patients with a conscious sense of guilt for things they have really done.

207

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195031207-208DA Postgraduate Psychoanalytic Training Program: its Evolution, Principles and Operation at the New York Medical CollegeBy Alexander Gralnick, M.D. With a discussion remark by Franz Alexander, American J. of Psychiatry, 106, 11, May, 1950, pp. 841–844.MartinGrotjahn
responseSet.title: A Postgraduate Psychoanalytic Training Program: its Evolution, Principles and Operation at the New York Medical College: By Alexander Gralnick, M.D. With a discussion remark by Franz Alexander, American J. of Psychiatry, 106,  11, May, 1950, pp. 841–844.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Postgraduate Psychoanalytic Training Program: its Evolution, Principles and Operation at the New York Medical College

By Alexander Gralnick, M.D. With a discussion remark by Franz Alexander, American J. of Psychiatry, 106, 11, May, 1950, pp. 841–844.

Martin Grotjahn M.D.

Martin Grotjahn

Martin Grotjahn  

This paper is a brief outline of the present tendency in America to combine psycho-analytic training with the teaching in medical schools. According to the author the difficulties of psycho-analysis are partly due to the fact that instruction is conducted in private institutes rather than in medical schools 'free of dogmatic teaching'. An attempt at such a programme of graduate courses in psycho-analysis

207

has been made at the New York Medical College. Changes in psycho-analytic ideas must be accompanied by changes in educational forms and methods. Originally there was an urgent need for unaffiliated training, but this time is now gone by.

Alexander describes 'a cultural lag' in the development of psycho-analytic teaching. Psycho-analytic training had of necessity to develop in isolation from the rest of the medical programme, but the plan described by Gralnick is a good example of the gradual liquidation of the 'cultural lag'. In 1948, in Chicago, the Associated Psychiatry Faculties, Inc., was founded. Members of the Association are the psychiatric departments of the University of Chicago, the University of Illinois, Michael Reese Hospital, and the Chicago Psycho-analytic Institute. Its aim is the co-ordination of the general psychiatric and psycho-analytic training of the residents in these institutions, which is a joint responsibility of the Associated Psychiatric Faculties. The resistance of the medical school justifies the isolationism of psycho-analysis, and the isolationism of psycho-analysis vindicates the resistance of the medical schools. With the relaxation of uncritical rejection, the defenders of new ideas can become more self-critical, and tolerate differences of opinion among themselves without which no development in the field of science is possible.

208

M." listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>S.</nfirst><nmid>M.</nmid><nlast>Payne</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: Ruth D. Usher, M.A.(Cantab.), B.A.(Lond.), M.R.C.S., L.R.C.P.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ruth D. Usher, M.A.(Cantab.), B.A.(Lond.), M.R.C.S., L.R.C.P.

S. Payne

S. Payne

S. Payne  

The British Psycho-Analytical Society sustained a severe loss by the death of Dr. Ruth D. Usher in May, 1950. Dr. Usher had been a member of the Society since 1944, and in this short time had shown unusual ability both in scientific and executive work in connection with the Institute of Psycho-Analysis and the Society.

Her personality and mental equipment were characteristic of a group of British middle-class women born towards the end of the last century and privileged to be amongst the first women to receive a University education. The majority of women in this group were distinguished by character traits of integrity, independence and honesty combined with mental ability of a high order. The profound changes in the status and education of women and the social upheavals resulting from the two European wars have made this pioneer group of historical significance, and it can be regarded as transitional between the typical Victorian 'lady' and the average professional or business woman of the present day.

Ruth Usher was born in Bristol in 1891. She was the second of four girls and was educated at Clifton High School and Newnham College Cambridge, where she took a Mathematical Tripos in 1913. She taught in several girls' schools belonging to the Girls' Public Day School Company from 1913 to 1930, having taken a Cambridge Teachers Training Certificate in 1920. While taking her teaching certificate her interest in psychology was aroused, and in 1932 she decided to study psychology and took a B.A.

291

responseSet.documentRef: Foulkes, S. H. (1950) Psycho-Analysis: By Edward Glover.  (London and New York:  Staples Press, 1949. Pp. 352. 15 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis  31:294
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Foulkes, S. H. (1950) Psycho-Analysis: By Edward Glover. (London and New York: Staples Press, 1949. Pp. 352. 15 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 31:294
responseSet.documentRefXML: Foulkes, S. H. (1950) Psycho-Analysis: By Edward Glover. (London and New York: Staples Press, 1949. Pp. 352. 15 s.). International Journal of Psychoanalysis 31:294
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195031294APsycho-AnalysisBy Edward Glover. (London and New York: Staples Press, 1949. Pp. 352. 15 s.)S.H.Foulkes
responseSet.title: Psycho-Analysis: By Edward Glover.  (London and New York:  Staples Press, 1949. Pp. 352. 15 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psycho-Analysis

By Edward Glover. (London and New York: Staples Press, 1949. Pp. 352. 15 s.)

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes

S. Foulkes  

In this second edition of his book on psycho-Analysis, Dr. Glover has brought up to date and expanded his account of basic psycho-analytic theory, first published in 1939 and reviewed in Vol. XI of this Journal.

The new edition includes a chapter on psycho-somatic and allied disorders, a further list of books for study, a glossary of terms and a short review of current modifications of psycho-analytical therapy.

Written for the use of medical practitioners, it aims at giving them a modicum of psycho-analytic understanding, so that they may recommend for suitable treatment patients who consult them with psychological problems and recognize mental disturbance underlying cases of stubborn organic illness.

The book divides into three parts, dealing with the theory of psycho-analysis, clinical psycho-analysis, and practical applications.

In the first section Dr. Glover introduces the theory of normal mental function, prefacing his observations with an outline of mental development in the first five or six years of life. He emphasizes the stress in terms of libido and ego development which accompanies this early phase of growth and underlines the causative part played in adult neuroses by childhood disturbances.

In the chapters on clinical psycho-analysis which follow, he proceeds to equip his reader with the etiology of different varieties of mental disorder.

Although his intentions are avowedly nontechnical, Dr. Glover has not been able to avoid technical concepts altogether. Throughout the book he concentrates on the theoretical and metapsychological aspects of his subject. Topographic, dynamic and economic viewpoints are given full consideration, while at the same time these are always related to total mental function.

This is a well-balanced and clear exposition with adequate basic concepts. It presents psycho-analysis as a diagnostic, therapeutic and research instrument. Dr. Glover, as he says himself, avoids controversial views and theories, his purpose being to acquaint the lay reader with the established views generally accepted among analysts. His account of the nuclear theory of ego-development, however, and the stress laid on affects, tension affects and discharge affects, give the book a personal note and deserve the interest of all analysts.

responseSet.title: 'Women who Fall'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Women who Fall'11

John Leuba

John Leuba

John Leuba
PARIS

John Leuba  

There is a narcissistic syndrome which, as far as I know, has never been described clearly and which deserves special mention on account of the constancy of a symptom which characterizes it. So far, I have observed this syndrome only in women, and moreover, though I have recognized the same group of symptoms in several men patients, it was precisely the most characteristic symptom, namely stumbling, which was missing. So until more information is available, I shall not give this syndrome any special designation, but will merely include patients suffering from it, under the heading of 'women who fall'.

Stumbling, which may occur to the extent of sudden falls in places where no physical obstacle is present to justify them, is to be seen from the beginning of the second period of childhood and remains as a persistent symptom during adulthood. (Some of my patients near their forties show it in such a high degree that one of them, struck by the frequency with which she tripped, fell full length without apparent cause, or twisted her feet, consulted a neurologist, wondering whether she had some muscular or nervous disturbance. The doctor could find no neurological disorder, but on the contrary noted that she had the muscular tone of a sportswoman, and perfectly normal limb movement.)

I had paid no particular attention to the fact that the patient had told me, with much amusement, how she had fallen flat on a pavement while chatting with her husband, and so I had not been led to connect this fact with the particular features of the psychic structure of those patients who happened to relate incidentally during a session that they had tripped or had a fall in the street.

It was through a case under control that my attention was drawn to the inconsistent personality of a patient. Everything in this forty-year-old woman indicated ego weakness. One could discern only false motives for her behaviour, sham religious feeling and moral ideas, dismaying emotional needs without the most elementary notion of normal emotional relationships, quite infantile but strongly obsessive sexual impulses; but the whole resulted in an apparently coherent personality, as she was hemmed in by taboos and supported by anachronistic interpretations of moral rules.
